title: RB-075 — Kafka Consumer Backpressure on order-events
applies_when: Order confirmations lag, Kafka producer or consumer span latency grows, or notification-service consumer lag rises on the order-events topic.
content:
Symptom signature
- order-service Producer spans and notification-service Consumer spans (kind in Producer/Consumer, messaging.system=kafka, topic=order-events) show growing latency or growing offset lag between produce and consume on the same trace_id.
- Frequently a SECONDARY effect, not a primary cause: an upstream slowdown on checkout-service floods order-service with a burst of delayed requests, which shows up as order.processing_delayed=true and a fraud_detection_backpressure=true log line on order-service, and only then shows up as consumer lag on notification-service.

Root-cause discipline — read this before acting
Kafka lag in this stack is usually a symptom, not the cause. Before treating the consumer as the bottleneck, confirm whether an upstream service (most commonly checkout-service) is the actual origin of the incident. Check checkout-service's own p99 latency and error rate over the same time window. If checkout-service latency is already elevated, the correct response is to fix the upstream cause (see RB-050 for triage, or RB-101 if a synchronous third-party call such as FraudShield is involved) — the Kafka lag will drain on its own once the upstream burst subsides, and no Kafka-side remediation is needed.

Remediation — if checkout-service (or another upstream service) is confirmed as the origin
1. Fix the upstream cause first. Do not scale or restart the Kafka consumer as a first response — it will not address the root cause and may mask the real signal.

Remediation — if the consumer itself is confirmed as the genuine bottleneck (checkout-service latency is normal, only consumer lag is elevated)
1. Scale notification-service consumer instances, or raise partition-level parallelism, so more of the topic's partitions are actively being consumed in parallel.
2. Increase the consumer's max.poll.records and processing concurrency, and confirm message processing is non-blocking so a single slow message cannot stall the whole partition.
3. Add a dead-letter path so a poison message cannot indefinitely stall a partition's consumer.

Follow-up
4. Alert directly on consumer-group lag as its own signal, rather than only inferring it from end-to-end order-confirmation latency — this shortens time-to-detection the next time an upstream burst hits the topic.
